# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу навколо вас — це наступний великий крок. Прикметники дають можливість висловлювати думки та ділитися емоціями.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world around you is the next big step. Adjectives give you the ability to express thoughts and share emotions.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Welcome to the vibrant world of descriptive words! Up until now, you have learned how to name objects using nouns. You know how to identify people, places, and things. But what if you want to say that a city is big, a book is new, or the weather is beautiful? This is where adjectives come into play.

In Ukrainian, adjectives are the tools we use to add color, size, age, and quality to our nouns. They allow us to move from simply pointing at things to actually describing our feelings about them.

Це мі́сто.
Це га́рне мі́сто.

This is a city.
This is a beautiful city.

See the difference? The second sentence shares your opinion and creates a much richer conversation. However, there is a fundamental rule you must remember: adjectives are like mirrors. They must always reflect the characteristics of the noun they describe. This concept is called adjective agreement.

If you recall the "Gender Code" from our previous lessons, you know that every Ukrainian noun belongs to a specific category: masculine, feminine, or neuter. Because adjectives must agree with their nouns, a single descriptive word will change its ending depending on the gender of the noun it is attached to.

To ask for a description, we use the question word "which" or "what kind". Because of gender agreement, this question word also has different forms.

Яки́й?
Яка́?
Яке́?
Які́?

Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns.

Let us consider a cultural example. If you visit Kyiv, you will surely see St. Sophia's Cathedral. We call it «Софі́йський собо́р». How would we describe it? We can use words like «стари́й» and «вели́кий».

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

In Ukrainian, the vast majority of adjectives belong to the "Hard Stem" group. This simply means that the core part of the word ends in a hard consonant, and the endings follow a very predictable, standard pattern.

Let us look at the standard endings for hard stem adjectives. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім | new house |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га | new book |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́ | new window |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́ | new words |

When you look up an adjective in a dictionary, you will always find it listed in its masculine singular form ending in **-ий**.

> [!warning]
> Багато студентів роблять помилку. Вони використовують словникову форму для всіх слів. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це слово жіночого роду.
>
> Many students make a mistake. They use the dictionary form for all words. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is a feminine word.

You must change the adjective to match the feminine noun. The correct phrase is «нова́ маши́на».

Let us practice with some high-frequency opposites. These words are incredibly useful for daily descriptions.

вели́кий / мали́й
до́брий / пога́ний
стари́й / нови́й

big / small
good / bad
old / new

Here are some examples of these adjectives in action:

Це вели́ке мі́сто.
Це мали́й бі́знес.
Це до́брий ве́чір.
Це пога́на пого́да.
Це до́бра люди́на.
Це ціка́ва кни́га.

This is a big city.
This is small business.
This is a good evening.
This is bad weather.
This is a kind person.
This is an interesting book.

You can use adjectives in two different positions in a sentence. You can place the adjective directly before the noun, which is called the attributive position.

Це га́рна пого́да.

This is beautiful weather.

Alternatively, you can place the adjective after the noun to make a statement, which is called the predicative position. Because Ukrainian often drops the "to be" verb in the present tense, we just use a dash or a pause.

Пого́да — га́рна.

The weather is beautiful.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender, regardless of where it sits in the sentence.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

While most adjectives belong to the hard stem group, there is a smaller but important category called "Soft Stem" adjectives. For these words, the core stem ends in a soft consonant.

Because the consonant is soft, the vowels in the endings must also be "soft". We use vowels that naturally follow soft sounds.

Let us look at the endings for the soft stem group. We will use the word «си́ній» as our primary example.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір | blue color |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія | blue line |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре | blue sea |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́ | blue tickets |

> [!tip]
> Якщо ви використовуєте метро в Києві, ви бачите різні лінії. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад м'якої групи.
>
> If you use the metro in Kyiv, you see different lines. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example of the soft stem group.

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect. You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending.

The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та
нові́ дні
вели́кі міста́
старі́ книжки́

beautiful girls
new days
big cities
old books

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Now that you understand how adjective endings work, let us put this knowledge into practice by looking at some cultural and practical contexts.

First, let us consider a famous figure from Ukrainian folklore. Mavka («Ма́вка») is a mythological forest spirit, famously depicted in Lesya Ukrainka's classic play "The Forest Song".

How would we describe Mavka? Since «Ма́вка» is a feminine noun, we must use feminine adjectives ending in **-а** or **-я**.

молода́ Ма́вка
га́рна дівчи́на
ціка́ва істо́рія
зеле́на су́кня

young Mavka
beautiful girl
interesting story
green dress

By combining these words, we build a vivid, descriptive picture of this cultural icon without needing any complex grammar.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка має зв'язок з природою. Її сукня — зеле́на. Слово «зеле́ний» має тверду основу. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka has a connection to nature. Her dress is green. The word for "green" has a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Next, let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces.

Це нова́ кварти́ра.
Це вели́кий буди́нок.
Це дорога́ маши́на.
Це деше́ва кімна́та.
Яке́ га́рне вікно́!

This is a new apartment.
This is a big house.
This is an expensive car.
This is a cheap room.
What a beautiful window!

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». This is the core of descriptive fluency in Ukrainian.

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

In this lesson, we discovered how adjectives bring the Ukrainian language to life. Remember the golden rule: an adjective must always agree with the gender and number of the noun it describes.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. To run a command in the background, set the `is_background` parameter to true. Do NOT use `&` to background commands. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group. Command process group can be terminated as `kill -- -PGID` or signaled as `kill -s SIGNAL -- -PGID`.

      Efficiency Guidelines:
      - Quiet Flags: Always prefer silent or quiet flags (e.g., `npm install --silent`, `git --no-pager`) to reduce output volume while still capturing necessary information.
      - Pagination: Always disable terminal pagination to ensure commands terminate (e.g., use `git --no-pager`, `systemctl --no-pager`, or set `PAGER=cat`).

      The following information is returned:

      Output: Combined stdout/stderr. Can be `(empty)` or partial on error and for any unwaited background processes.
      Exit Code: Only included if non-zero (command failed).
      Error: Only included if a process-level error occurred (e.g., spawn failure).
      Signal: Only included if process was terminated by a signal.
      Background PIDs: Only included if background processes were started.
      Process Group PGID: Only included if available.

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Adjective agreement in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter) and number (plural) in the Nominative case, hard and soft stems.
Not covered:
  - Colors and Clothing → a1-12
  - Description: Adverbs → a1-42
-->

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу — це наступний крок. Прикметники — це нові можливості.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world is the next step. Adjectives are new possibilities.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Welcome to the vibrant world of descriptive words! Up until now, you have learned how to name objects using nouns. You know how to identify people, places, and things. But what if you want to say that a city is big, a book is new, or the weather is beautiful? This is where adjectives come into play.

Прикметники — це кольори. Це розмір. Це вік. Це якість.

Adjectives are colors. This is size. This is age. This is quality.

They allow us to move from simply pointing at things to actually describing our feelings about them.

Це мі́сто. Це га́рне мі́сто.

A city. A beautiful city.

See the difference? The second sentence shares your opinion and creates a much richer conversation. However, there is a fundamental rule you must remember: adjectives are like mirrors. They must always reflect the characteristics of the noun they describe. This concept is called adjective agreement.

Кожне слово має рід. Це чоловічий рід. Це жіночий рід. Це середній рід.

Every word has a gender. This is masculine. This is feminine. This is neuter.

Because adjectives must agree with their nouns, a single descriptive word will change its ending depending on the gender of the noun it is attached to. To ask for a description, we use the question word "which" or "what kind". Because of gender agreement, this question word also has different forms.

Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Let us consider a cultural example. If you visit Kyiv, you will surely see St. Sophia's Cathedral.

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

In Ukrainian, the vast majority of adjectives belong to the "Hard Stem" group. This simply means that the core part of the word ends in a hard consonant, and the endings follow a very predictable, standard pattern.

Let us look at the standard endings for hard stem adjectives. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

When you look up an adjective in a dictionary, you will always find it listed in its masculine singular form ending in **-ий**.

> [!warning]
> Студенти роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

You must change the adjective to match the feminine noun. The correct phrase is «нова́ маши́на».

Let us practice with some high-frequency opposites. These words are incredibly useful for daily descriptions.

вели́кий / мали́й.
до́брий / пога́ний.
стари́й / нови́й.

big / small.
good / bad.
old / new.

Here are some examples of these adjectives in action:

Це вели́ке мі́сто. (A big city.)
Це мали́й бі́знес. (A small business.)
Це до́брий ве́чір. (A good evening.)
Це пога́на пого́да. (Bad weather.)
Це до́бра люди́на. (A kind person.)
Це ціка́ва кни́га. (An interesting book.)

You can use adjectives in two different positions in a sentence. You can place the adjective directly before the noun, which is called the attributive position.

Це га́рна пого́да.

This is beautiful weather.

Alternatively, you can place the adjective after the noun to make a statement, which is called the predicative position. Because Ukrainian often drops the "to be" verb in the present tense, we just use a dash or a pause.

Пого́да — га́рна.

The weather is beautiful.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender, regardless of where it sits in the sentence.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

While most adjectives belong to the hard stem group, there is a smaller but important category called "Soft Stem" adjectives. For these words, the core stem ends in a soft consonant.

Because the consonant is soft, the vowels in the endings must also be "soft". We use vowels that naturally follow soft sounds.

Let us look at the endings for the soft stem group. We will use the word «си́ній» as our primary example.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |

> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect. You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending.

The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та.
нові́ дні.
вели́кі міста́.
старі́ книжки́.

beautiful girls.
new days.
big cities.
old books.

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Now that you understand how adjective endings work, let us put this knowledge into practice by looking at some cultural and practical contexts.

First, let us consider a famous figure from Ukrainian folklore. Mavka («Ма́вка») is a mythological forest spirit, famously depicted in Lesya Ukrainka's classic play "The Forest Song".

How would we describe Mavka? Since «Ма́вка» is a feminine noun, we must use feminine adjectives ending in **-а** or **-я**.

молода́ Ма́вка.
га́рна дівчи́на.
ціка́ва істо́рія.
зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka.
A beautiful girl.
An interesting story.
A green dress.

By combining these words, we build a vivid, descriptive picture of this cultural icon without needing any complex grammar.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Next, let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces. Let us look at a short dialogue.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Який це будинок?
— Це новий будинок.
— Яка це квартира?
— Це велика квартира.
— А вікно?
— Це гарне вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— It is a new house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— It is a big apartment.
— And the window?
— It is a beautiful window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». This is the core of descriptive fluency in Ukrainian.

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

In this lesson, we discovered how adjectives bring the Ukrainian language to life. Remember the golden rule: an adjective must always agree with the gender and number of the noun it describes.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

<!-- SCOPE

Not covered:
  - Colors and Clothing → a1-12

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу навколо вас — це наступний великий крок. Прикметники дають можливість висловлювати думки та ділитися емоціями.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world around you is the next big step. Adjectives give you the ability to express thoughts and share emotions.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Welcome to the vibrant world of descriptive words! Up until now, you have learned how to name objects using nouns. You know how to identify people, places, and things. But what if you want to say that a city is big, a book is new, or the weather is beautiful? This is where adjectives come into play.

In Ukrainian, adjectives are the tools we use to add color, size, age, and quality to our nouns. They allow us to move from simply pointing at things to actually describing our feelings about them.

Це мі́сто.
Це га́рне мі́сто.

This is a city.
This is a beautiful city.

See the difference? The second sentence shares your opinion and creates a much richer conversation. However, there is a fundamental rule you must remember: adjectives are like mirrors. They must always reflect the characteristics of the noun they describe. This concept is called adjective agreement.

If you recall the "Gender Code" from our previous lessons, you know that every Ukrainian noun belongs to a specific category: masculine, feminine, or neuter. Because adjectives must agree with their nouns, a single descriptive word will change its ending depending on the gender of the noun it is attached to.

To ask for a description, we use the question word "which" or "what kind". Because of gender agreement, this question word also has different forms.

Яки́й?
Яка́?
Яке́?
Які́?

Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns.
Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns.

Let us consider a cultural example. If you visit Kyiv, you will surely see St. Sophia's Cathedral. We call it «Софі́йський собо́р». How would we describe it? We can use words like «стари́й» and «вели́кий».

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

In Ukrainian, the vast majority of adjectives belong to the "Hard Stem" group. This simply means that the core part of the word ends in a hard consonant, and the endings follow a very predictable, standard pattern.

Let us look at the standard endings for hard stem adjectives. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім | new house |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га | new book |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́ | new window |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́ | new words |

When you look up an adjective in a dictionary, you will always find it listed in its masculine singular form ending in **-ий**.

> [!warning]
> Багато студентів роблять помилку. Вони використовують словникову форму для всіх слів. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це слово жіночого роду.
>
> Many students make a mistake. They use the dictionary form for all words. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is a feminine word.

You must change the adjective to match the feminine noun. The correct phrase is «нова́ маши́на».

Let us practice with some high-frequency opposites. These words are incredibly useful for daily descriptions.

вели́кий / мали́й
до́брий / пога́ний
стари́й / нови́й

big / small
good / bad
old / new

Here are some examples of these adjectives in action:

Це вели́ке мі́сто.
Це мали́й бі́знес.
Це до́брий ве́чір.
Це пога́на пого́да.
Це до́бра люди́на.
Це ціка́ва кни́га.

This is a big city.
This is small business.
This is a good evening.
This is bad weather.
This is a kind person.
This is an interesting book.

You can use adjectives in two different positions in a sentence. You can place the adjective directly before the noun, which is called the attributive position.

Це га́рна пого́да.

This is beautiful weather.

Alternatively, you can place the adjective after the noun to make a statement, which is called the predicative position. Because Ukrainian often drops the "to be" verb in the present tense, we just use a dash or a pause.

Пого́да — га́рна.

The weather is beautiful.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender, regardless of where it sits in the sentence.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

While most adjectives belong to the hard stem group, there is a smaller but important category called "Soft Stem" adjectives. For these words, the core stem ends in a soft consonant.

Because the consonant is soft, the vowels in the endings must also be "soft". We use vowels that naturally follow soft sounds.

Let us look at the endings for the soft stem group. We will use the word «си́ній» as our primary example.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір | blue color |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія | blue line |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре | blue sea |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́ | blue tickets |

> [!tip]
> Якщо ви використовуєте метро в Києві, ви бачите різні лінії. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад м'якої групи.
>
> If you use the metro in Kyiv, you see different lines. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example of the soft stem group.

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect. You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending.

The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та
нові́ дні
вели́кі міста́
старі́ книжки́

beautiful girls
new days
big cities
old books

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Now that you understand how adjective endings work, let us put this knowledge into practice by looking at some cultural and practical contexts.

First, let us consider a famous figure from Ukrainian folklore. Mavka («Ма́вка») is a mythological forest spirit, famously depicted in Lesya Ukrainka's classic play "The Forest Song".

How would we describe Mavka? Since «Ма́вка» is a feminine noun, we must use feminine adjectives ending in **-а** or **-я**.

молода́ Ма́вка
га́рна дівчи́на
ціка́ва істо́рія
зеле́на су́кня

young Mavka
beautiful girl
interesting story
green dress

By combining these words, we build a vivid, descriptive picture of this cultural icon without needing any complex grammar.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка має зв'язок з природою. Її сукня — зеле́на. Слово «зеле́ний» має тверду основу. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka has a connection to nature. Her dress is green. The word for "green" has a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Next, let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces.

Це нова́ кварти́ра.
Це вели́кий буди́нок.
Це дорога́ маши́на.
Це деше́ва кімна́та.
Яке́ га́рне вікно́!

This is a new apartment.
This is a big house.
This is an expensive car.
This is a cheap room.
What a beautiful window!

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». This is the core of descriptive fluency in Ukrainian.

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

In this lesson, we discovered how adjectives bring the Ukrainian language to life. Remember the golden rule: an adjective must always agree with the gender and number of the noun it describes.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

In this lesson, we discovered how adjectives bring the Ukrainian language to life. Remember the golden rule: an adjective must always agree with the gender and number of the noun it describes.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Adjective agreement in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter) and number (plural) in the Nominative case, hard and soft stems.
Not covered:
  - Colors and Clothing → a1-12
  - Description: Adverbs → a1-42
-->

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу навколо нас — це наступний крок. Прикметники — це нові можливості.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world around us is the next step. Adjectives are new possibilities.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Прикметники — це слова для опису. Це кольори. Це розмір. Це вік. Це якість.

Adjectives are words for description. This is colors. This is size. This is age. This is quality.

Вони дають можливість висловлювати думки. Це мі́сто. Це га́рне мі́сто.

They give the ability to express thoughts. A city. A beautiful city.

Кожне слово має рід. Це чоловічий рід. Це жіночий рід. Це середній рід.

Every word has a gender. This is masculine. This is feminine. This is neuter.

Прикметник завжди має такий самий рід, як іменник. Це правило.

The adjective always has the same gender as the noun. This is a rule.

Ми використовуємо питання «який». Воно має різні форми:
Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р. Це дуже цікаве місце.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral. This is a very interesting place.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

Більшість прикметників — це тверда група. Вони мають стандартні закінчення.

Most adjectives are the hard stem group. They have standard endings.

Let us look at the standard endings. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Category | Ending | Ukrainian Phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

У словнику слово має форму чоловічого роду. Це закінчення -ий.

In the dictionary, the word has the masculine form. This is the ending -ий.

> [!warning]
> Студенти часто роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students often make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

Правильна фраза — це «нова́ маши́на». Це нова квартира. Це нове життя.

The correct phrase is "a new car". This is a new apartment. This is a new life.

Це вели́ке мі́сто. Це мали́й бі́знес. Це до́брий ве́чір. Це пога́на пого́да. Це до́бра люди́на. Це ціка́ва кни́га.

A big city. A small business. A good evening. Bad weather. A kind person. An interesting book.

Це смачний борщ. Це червоний колір. Це дорогий телефон. Це дешева ручка.

This is a tasty borscht. This is a red color. This is an expensive phone. This is a cheap pen.

Прикметник може бути перед іменником. Це га́рна пого́да.

The adjective can be before the noun. This is beautiful weather.

Прикметник може бути після іменника. Пого́да — га́рна. Борщ — смачний. 

The adjective can be after the noun. The weather is beautiful. The borscht is tasty.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

Існує інша група прикметників. Це м'яка група.

There exists another group of adjectives. This is the soft stem group.

Вони мають м'які закінчення. Головний приклад — це слово «синій».

They have soft endings. The main example is the word "blue".

| Group | Soft Ending | Soft Example | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |

> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

Існує орфографічна пастка. Люди пишуть «си́ний». Це неправильно.

There is a spelling trap. People write «си́ний». This is incorrect.

You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending. The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth.

Множина завжди має закінчення -і. Це однакове закінчення для всіх прикметників.

The plural always has the ending -і. This is the same ending for all adjectives.

га́рні дівча́та. нові́ дні. вели́кі міста́. старі́ книжки́. си́ні зо́шити. добрі друзі.

beautiful girls. new days. big cities. old books. blue notebooks. good friends.

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Ма́вка — це персонаж української міфології. Ма́вка — це жіночий рід.

Mavka is a character of Ukrainian mythology. Mavka is feminine.

молода́ Ма́вка. га́рна дівчи́на. ціка́ва істо́рія. зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka. A beautiful girl. An interesting story. A green dress.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Ми маємо нову ситуацію. Ми в агенції нерухомості.

We have a new situation. We are in a real estate agency.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Яки́й це буди́нок?
— Це нови́й буди́нок. Це дорогий дім.
— Яка́ це кварти́ра?
— Це вели́ка кварти́ра. Це гарна кімната.
— А вікно́?
— Це га́рне вікно́. Це нове вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— It is a new house. It is an expensive house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— It is a big apartment. It is a beautiful room.
— And the window?
— It is a beautiful window. It is a new window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». 

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

Прикметники роблять мову багатою. Головне правило: прикметник і іменник мають однаковий рід і число.

Adjectives make the language rich. The main rule: the adjective and the noun have the same gender and number.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

Прикметник завжди має такий самий рід, як іменник. Це правило.

The adjective always has the same gender as the noun. This is a rule.

Ми використовуємо питання «який». Воно має різні форми:
Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р. Це дуже цікаве місце.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral. This is a very interesting place.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

Більшість прикметників — це тверда група. Вони мають стандартні закінчення.

Most adjectives are the hard stem group. They have standard endings.

Let us look at the standard endings. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Category | Ending | Ukrainian Phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

У словнику слово має форму чоловічого роду. Це закінчення -ий.

In the dictionary, the word has the masculine form. This is the ending -ий.

> [!warning]
> Студенти часто роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students often make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

Правильна фраза — це «нова́ маши́на». Це нова квартира. Це нове життя.

The correct phrase is "a new car". This is a new apartment. This is a new life.

Це вели́ке мі́сто. Це мали́й бі́знес. Це до́брий ве́чір. Це пога́на пого́да. Це до́бра люди́на. Це ціка́ва кни́га.

A big city. A small business. A good evening. Bad weather. A kind person. An interesting book.

Це смачний борщ. Це червоний колір. Це дорогий телефон. Це дешева ручка.

This is a tasty borscht. This is a red color. This is an expensive phone. This is a cheap pen.

Прикметник може бути перед іменником. Це га́рна пого́да.

The adjective can be before the noun. This is beautiful weather.

Прикметник може бути після іменника. Пого́да — га́рна. Борщ — смачний. 

The adjective can be after the noun. The weather is beautiful. The borscht is tasty.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

Існує інша група прикметників. Це м'яка група.

There exists another group of adjectives. This is the soft stem group.

Вони мають м'які закінчення. Головний приклад — це слово «синій».

They have soft endings. The main example is the word "blue".

| Group | Soft Ending | Soft Example | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |
...
> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

Існує орфографічна пастка. Люди пишуть «си́ний». Це неправильно.

There is a spelling trap. People write «си́ний». This is incorrect.

You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending. The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth.

Множина завжди має закінчення -і. Це однакове закінчення для всіх прикметників.

The plural always has the ending -і. This is the same ending for all adjectives.

га́рні дівча́та. нові́ дні. вели́кі міста́. старі́ книжки́. си́ні зо́шити. добрі друзі.

beautiful girls. new days. big cities. old books. blue notebooks. good friends.

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Ма́вка — це персонаж української міфології. Ма́вка — це жіночий рід.

Mavka is a character of Ukrainian mythology. Mavka is feminine.

молода́ Ма́вка. га́рна дівчи́на. ціка́ва істо́рія. зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka. A beautiful girl. An interesting story. A green dress.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Ми маємо нову ситуацію. Ми в агенції нерухомості.

We have a new situation. We are in a real estate agency.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Яки́й це буди́нок?
— Це нови́й буди́нок. Це дорогий дім.
— Яка́ це кварти́ра?
— Це вели́ка кварти́ра. Це гарна кімната.
— А вікно́?
— Це га́рне вікно́. Це нове вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— It is a new house. It is an expensive house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— It is a big apartment. It is a beautiful room.
— And the window?
— It is a beautiful window. It is a new window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». 

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

Прикметники роблять мову багатою. Головне правило: прикметник і іменник мають однаковий рід і число.

Adjectives make the language rich. The main rule: the adjective and the noun have the same gender and number.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
...

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу — це наступний крок. Прикметники — це нові можливості.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world is the next step. Adjectives are new possibilities.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Welcome to the vibrant world of descriptive words! Up until now, you have learned how to name objects using nouns. You know how to identify people, places, and things. But what if you want to say that a city is big, a book is new, or the weather is beautiful? This is where adjectives come into play.

Прикметники — це кольори. Це розмір. Це вік. Це якість.

Adjectives are colors. This is size. This is age. This is quality.

They allow us to move from simply pointing at things to actually describing our feelings about them.

Це мі́сто. Це га́рне мі́сто.

A city. A beautiful city.

See the difference? The second sentence shares your opinion and creates a much richer conversation. However, there is a fundamental rule you must remember: adjectives are like mirrors. They must always reflect the characteristics of the noun they describe. This concept is called adjective agreement.

Кожне слово має рід. Це чоловічий рід. Це жіночий рід. Це середній рід.

Every word has a gender. This is masculine. This is feminine. This is neuter.

Because adjectives must agree with their nouns, a single descriptive word will change its ending depending on the gender of the noun it is attached to. To ask for a description, we use the question word "which" or "what kind". Because of gender agreement, this question word also has different forms.

Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Let us consider a cultural example. If you visit Kyiv, you will surely see St. Sophia's Cathedral.

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

In Ukrainian, the vast majority of adjectives belong to the "Hard Stem" group. This simply means that the core part of the word ends in a hard consonant, and the endings follow a very predictable, standard pattern.

Let us look at the standard endings for hard stem adjectives. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

When you look up an adjective in a dictionary, you will always find it listed in its masculine singular form ending in **-ий**.

> [!warning]
> Студенти роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

You must change the adjective to match the feminine noun. The correct phrase is «нова́ маши́на».

Let us practice with some high-frequency opposites. These words are incredibly useful for daily descriptions.

вели́кий / мали́й.
до́брий / пога́ний.
стари́й / нови́й.

big / small.
good / bad.
old / new.

Here are some examples of these adjectives in action:

Це вели́ке мі́сто. (A big city.)
Це мали́й бі́знес. (A small business.)
Це до́брий ве́чір. (A good evening.)
Це пога́на пого́да. (Bad weather.)
Це до́бра люди́на. (A kind person.)
Це ціка́ва кни́га. (An interesting book.)

You can use adjectives in two different positions in a sentence. You can place the adjective directly before the noun, which is called the attributive position.

Це га́рна пого́да.

This is beautiful weather.

Alternatively, you can place the adjective after the noun to make a statement, which is called the predicative position. Because Ukrainian often drops the "to be" verb in the present tense, we just use a dash or a pause.

Пого́да — га́рна.

The weather is beautiful.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender, regardless of where it sits in the sentence.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

While most adjectives belong to the hard stem group, there is a smaller but important category called "Soft Stem" adjectives. For these words, the core stem ends in a soft consonant.

Because the consonant is soft, the vowels in the endings must also be "soft". We use vowels that naturally follow soft sounds.

Let us look at the endings for the soft stem group. We will use the word «си́ній» as our primary example.

| Gender | Ending | Example | Translation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |

> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect. You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending.

The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та.
нові́ дні.
вели́кі міста́.
старі́ книжки́.

beautiful girls.
new days.
big cities.
old books.

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Now that you understand how adjective endings work, let us put this knowledge into practice by looking at some cultural and practical contexts.

First, let us consider a famous figure from Ukrainian folklore. Mavka («Ма́вка») is a mythological forest spirit, famously depicted in Lesya Ukrainka's classic play "The Forest Song".

How would we describe Mavka? Since «Ма́вка» is a feminine noun, we must use feminine adjectives ending in **-а** or **-я**.

молода́ Ма́вка.
га́рна дівчи́на.
ціка́ва істо́рія.
зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka.
A beautiful girl.
An interesting story.
A green dress.

By combining these words, we build a vivid, descriptive picture of this cultural icon without needing any complex grammar.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Next, let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces. Let us look at a short dialogue.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Який це будинок?
— Це новий будинок.
— Яка це квартира?
— Це велика квартира.
— А вікно?
— Це гарне вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— It is a new house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— It is a big apartment.
— And the window?
— It is a beautiful window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». This is the core of descriptive fluency in Ukrainian.

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

In this lesson, we discovered how adjectives bring the Ukrainian language to life. Remember the golden rule: an adjective must always agree with the gender and number of the noun it describes.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу навколо нас — це наступний крок. Прикметники — це нові можливості.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world around us is the next step. Adjectives are new possibilities.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Прикметники — це слова для опису. Це кольори. Це розмір. Це вік. Це якість.

Adjectives are words for description. This is colors. This is size. This is age. This is quality.

Вони дають можливість висловлювати думки. Це мі́сто. Це га́рне мі́сто.

They give the ability to express thoughts. A city. A beautiful city.

Кожне слово має рід. Це чоловічий рід. Це жіночий рід. Це середній рід.

Every word has a gender. This is masculine. This is feminine. This is neuter.

Прикметник завжди має такий самий рід, як іменник. Це правило.

The adjective always has the same gender as the noun. This is a rule.

Ми використовуємо питання «який». Воно має різні форми:
Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р. Це дуже цікаве місце.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral. This is a very interesting place.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

Більшість прикметників — це тверда група. Вони мають стандартні закінчення.

Most adjectives are the hard stem group. They have standard endings.

Let us look at the standard endings. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Category | Ending | Ukrainian Phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

У словнику слово має форму чоловічого роду. Це закінчення -ий.

In the dictionary, the word has the masculine form. This is the ending -ий.

> [!warning]
> Студенти часто роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students often make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

Правильна фраза — це «нова́ маши́на». Це нова квартира. Це нове життя.

The correct phrase is "a new car". This is a new apartment. This is a new life.

Це вели́ке мі́сто. Це мали́й бі́знес. Це до́брий ве́чір. Це пога́на пого́да. Це до́бра люди́на. Це ціка́ва кни́га.

A big city. A small business. A good evening. Bad weather. A kind person. An interesting book.

Це смачний борщ. Це червоний колір. Це дорогий телефон. Це дешева ручка.

This is a tasty borscht. This is a red color. This is an expensive phone. This is a cheap pen.

Прикметник може бути перед іменником. Це га́рна пого́да.

The adjective can be before the noun. This is beautiful weather.

Прикметник може бути після іменника. Пого́да — га́рна. Борщ — смачний. 

The adjective can be after the noun. The weather is beautiful. The borscht is tasty.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

Існує інша група прикметників. Це м'яка група.

There exists another group of adjectives. This is the soft stem group.

Вони мають м'які закінчення. Головний приклад — це слово «синій».

They have soft endings. The main example is the word "blue".

| Group | Soft Ending | Soft Example | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |

> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

Існує орфографічна пастка. Люди пишуть «си́ний». Це неправильно.

There is a spelling trap. People write «си́ний». This is incorrect.

You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending. The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth.

Множина завжди має закінчення -і. Це однакове закінчення для всіх прикметників.

The plural always has the ending -і. This is the same ending for all adjectives.

га́рні дівча́та. нові́ дні. вели́кі міста́. старі́ книжки́. си́ні зо́шити. добрі друзі.

beautiful girls. new days. big cities. old books. blue notebooks. good friends.

Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Ма́вка — це персонаж української міфології. Ма́вка — це жіночий рід.

Mavka is a character of Ukrainian mythology. Mavka is feminine.

молода́ Ма́вка. га́рна дівчи́на. ціка́ва істо́рія. зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka. A beautiful girl. An interesting story. A green dress.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Ми маємо нову ситуацію. Ми в агенції нерухомості.

We have a new situation. We are in a real estate agency.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Яки́й це буди́нок?
— Це нови́й буди́нок. Це дорогий дім.
— Яка́ це кварти́ра?
— Це вели́ка кварти́ра. Це гарна кімната.
— А вікно́?
— Це га́рне вікно́. Це нове вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— It is a new house. It is an expensive house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— It is a big apartment. It is a beautiful room.
— And the window?
— It is a beautiful window. It is a new window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». 

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

Прикметники роблять мову багатою. Головне правило: прикметник і іменник мають однаковий рід і число.

Adjectives make the language rich. The main rule: the adjective and the noun have the same gender and number.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

args.content:
<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Adjective agreement in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter) and number (plural) in the Nominative case, hard and soft stems.
Not covered:
  - Colors and Clothing → a1-12
  - Description: Adverbs → a1-42
-->

# Describing Things - Adjectives

> **Чому це важливо?**
> Опис світу навколо нас — це важливий крок. Нові слова — це нові можливості.
>
> **Why does this matter?**
> Describing the world around us is an important step. New words are new possibilities.

## Вступ: Світ прикметників (Introduction: The World of Adjectives)

Welcome to the vibrant world of descriptive words! Up until now, you have learned how to name objects using nouns. You know how to identify people, places, and things. But what if you want to say that a city is big, a book is new, or the weather is beautiful? This is where adjectives come into play.

Adjectives are words used for description. They represent colors, sizes, ages, and qualities. They give you the ability to express thoughts and share opinions about your surroundings.

Це мі́сто. Це га́рне мі́сто.

A city. A beautiful city.

See the difference? The second sentence shares your opinion and creates a much richer conversation. Every noun has a specific gender category. 

Це чоловічий рід. Це жіночий рід. Це середній рід.

This is masculine. This is feminine. This is neuter.

The adjective always has the same gender as the noun it describes. This is a fundamental rule called adjective agreement. To ask for a description, we use the question word "which" or "what kind". Because of gender agreement, this question word also has different forms depending on the noun.

Яки́й? (Which? / What kind? — for masculine nouns).
Яка́? (Which? / What kind? — for feminine nouns).
Яке́? (Which? / What kind? — for neuter nouns).
Які́? (Which? / What kind? — for plural nouns).

Let us consider a cultural example. If you visit Kyiv, you will surely see St. Sophia's Cathedral.

Софі́йський собо́р — це стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий собо́р. Це цікаве місце.

St. Sophia's Cathedral is an old cathedral. It is a grand cathedral. A very interesting place.

> [!culture]
> Софі́йський собо́р — це дуже стари́й собо́р. Це вели́кий та га́рний собо́р у місті Київ. 
>
> St. Sophia's Cathedral is a very old cathedral. It is a grand and beautiful cathedral in the city of Kyiv.

Because «собо́р» is a masculine noun, our adjectives must also take the masculine form. In the next section, we will look exactly at how to form these specific endings.

## Тверда група прикметників (Hard Stem Adjectives)

In Ukrainian, the vast majority of adjectives belong to the "Hard Stem" group. This simply means that the core part of the word ends in a hard consonant, and the endings follow a very predictable, standard pattern.

Let us look at the standard endings for hard stem adjectives. Notice how the ending changes to match the gender of the noun.

| Category | Ending | Ukrainian Phrase | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ий** | нови́й дім. | a new house. |
| Feminine | **-а** | нова́ кни́га. | a new book. |
| Neuter | **-е** | нове́ вікно́. | a new window. |
| Plural | **-і** | нові́ слова́. | new words. |

When you look up an adjective in a dictionary, you will always find it listed in its masculine singular form ending in **-ий**.

> [!warning]
> Студенти часто роблять помилку. Вони кажуть «нови́й маши́на». Це помилка! Маши́на — це жіночий рід.
>
> Students often make a mistake. They say «нови́й маши́на». This is a mistake! Machine (car) is feminine.

You must change the adjective to match the feminine noun.

Правильна фраза — це «нова́ маши́на». Це нова квартира. Це нове життя.

The correct phrase is "a new car". A new apartment. A new life.

Let us practice with some high-frequency opposites. These words are incredibly useful for daily descriptions. You will use them constantly.

вели́кий / мали́й.
до́брий / пога́ний.
стари́й / нови́й.

big / small.
good / bad.
old / new.

Here are some examples of these adjectives in action:

Це вели́ке мі́сто. Це мали́й бі́знес. Це до́брий ве́чір. Це пога́на пого́да. Це до́бра люди́на. Це ціка́ва кни́га.

A big city. A small business. A good evening. Bad weather. A kind person. An interesting book.

Це смачний борщ. Це червоний колір. Це дорогий телефон. Це дешева ручка.

A tasty borscht. A red color. An expensive phone. A cheap pen.

An adjective can be placed directly before the noun. This is called the attributive position. In this position, the adjective acts as a direct modifier to the noun.

Це га́рна пого́да.

A beautiful weather.

An adjective can also be placed after the noun. This is called the predicative position. Because Ukrainian often drops the verb "to be" in the present tense, we just use a dash or a pause to connect the noun and its description.

Пого́да — га́рна. Борщ — смачний. 

The weather is beautiful. The borscht is tasty.

Notice that in both cases, the adjective ending remains exactly the same. It must always agree with the noun's gender, regardless of its position in the sentence.

## М'яка група та множина (Soft Stem and Plurals)

While most adjectives belong to the hard stem group, there exists another smaller but important category. This is the soft stem group.

For these words, the core stem ends in a soft consonant. Because the consonant is soft, the vowels in the endings must also be "soft". We use vowels that naturally follow soft sounds.

Let us look at the endings for the soft stem group. We will use the word «си́ній» as our primary example.

| Group | Soft Ending | Soft Example | English |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masculine | **-ій** | си́ній ко́лір. | blue color. |
| Feminine | **-я** | си́ня лі́нія. | blue line. |
| Neuter | **-є** | си́нє мо́ре. | blue sea. |
| Plural | **-і** | си́ні квитки́. | blue tickets. |

> [!tip]
> Київське метро. Си́ня лі́нія — це важливий маршрут. Це ідеальний приклад.
>
> Kyiv metro. The blue line is an important route. This is a perfect example.

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect.

You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending. The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending. This small phonetic detail makes a big difference in how natural your pronunciation sounds.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та. нові́ дні. вели́кі міста́. старі́ книжки́. си́ні зо́шити. добрі друзі.

beautiful girls. new days. big cities. old books. blue notebooks. good friends.

Notice how the plural ending unifies everything. You do not need to memorize separate plural endings for masculine, feminine, or neuter nouns. The **-і** ending is universal for plurals. Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Ма́вка — це персонаж української міфології. Ма́вка — це жіночий рід.

Mavka is a character of Ukrainian mythology. Mavka is feminine.

молода́ Ма́вка. га́рна дівчи́на. ціка́ва істо́рія. зеле́на су́кня.

A young Mavka. A beautiful girl. An interesting story. A green dress.

> [!did-you-know]
> В українській міфології Мавка — це ліс. Її сукня — зеле́на. Це тверда основа. Жіноча форма — це «зеле́на».
>
> In Ukrainian mythology, Mavka is the forest. Her dress is green. This is a hard stem. The feminine form is "green".

Ми маємо нову ситуацію. Ми в агенції нерухомості.

We have a new situation. We are in a real estate agency.

Let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces. Let us look at a short dialogue.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Яки́й це буди́нок?
— Це нови́й буди́нок. Це дорогий дім.
— Яка́ це кварти́ра?
— Це вели́ка кварти́ра. Це гарна кімната.
— А вікно́?
— Це га́рне вікно́. Це нове вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— A new house. An expensive house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— A big apartment. A beautiful room.
— And the window?
— A beautiful window. A new window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». 

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

Adjectives make the language rich. The main rule: the adjective and the noun have the same gender and number.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:

Яки́й?
Which? / What kind? - for a masculine noun.
Відповідь: нови́й телефо́н (new phone).

Яка́?
Which? / What kind? - for a feminine noun.
Відповідь: вели́ка кни́га (big book).

Яке́?
Which? / What kind? - for a neuter noun.
Відповідь: си́нє мо́ре (blue sea).

Які́?
Which? / What kind? - for plural nouns.
Відповідь: га́рні кві́ти (beautiful flowers).

There is a common spelling trap here. English speakers often try to spell the masculine form of "blue" as «си́ний», using the hard **-ий** ending because it sounds similar to their ears. However, this is orthographically incorrect.

You must always write «си́ній» with the soft **-ій** ending. The soft sign or the soft vowel changes the sound of the previous consonant. For English speakers, practicing the soft **-ій** ending can be tricky at first. Take your time and make sure you raise your tongue towards the roof of your mouth when pronouncing the soft ending. This small phonetic detail makes a big difference in how natural your pronunciation sounds.

Now, let us talk about the plural form. You might have noticed something interesting when comparing the hard and soft groups.

For both hard stem adjectives and soft stem adjectives, the plural ending is exactly the same. It is always **-і**.

This makes describing multiple objects wonderfully simple. You do not need to worry about whether the original noun was masculine, feminine, or neuter. If the noun is plural, the adjective takes the **-і** ending.

га́рні дівча́та. нові́ дні. вели́кі міста́. старі́ книжки́. си́ні зо́шити. добрі друзі.

beautiful girls. new days. big cities. old books. blue notebooks. good friends.

Notice how the plural ending unifies everything. You do not need to memorize separate plural endings for masculine, feminine, or neuter nouns. The **-і** ending is universal for plurals. Remember that plural adjectives always take the **-і** ending, providing a welcome consistency as you expand your vocabulary.

## Практика: Люди і місця (Practice: People and Places)

Ма́вка — це персонаж української міфології. Ма́вка — це жіночий рід.

...

Ми маємо нову ситуацію. Ми в агенції нерухомості.

We have a new situation. We are in a real estate agency.

Let us imagine you are a real estate agent showing properties in Ukraine. You will need to describe buildings and apartments to your clients. We can use adjectives to highlight the qualities of different spaces. Let us look at a short dialogue.

Діалог: Нова квартира.
— Яки́й це буди́нок?
— Це нови́й буди́нок. Це дорогий дім.
— Яка́ це кварти́ра?
— Це вели́ка кварти́ра. Це гарна кімната.
— А вікно́?
— Це га́рне вікно́. Це нове вікно.

Dialogue: A new apartment.
— What kind of house is this?
— A new house. An expensive house.
— What kind of apartment is this?
— A big apartment. A beautiful room.
— And the window?
— A beautiful window. A new window.

Notice how the adjectives naturally shift to match the gender of each property type. The feminine «кварти́ра» takes «нова́», while the masculine «буди́нок» takes «вели́кий». 

## Підсумок (Summary & Self-Check)

Adjectives make the language rich. The main rule: the adjective and the noun have the same gender and number.

We use the question words «Яки́й?», «Яка́?», «Яке́?», and «Які́?» to ask for descriptions. Hard stem adjectives take the endings **-ий**, **-а**, **-е**, and **-і**, while soft stem adjectives take **-ій**, **-я**, **-є**, and **-і**. Fortunately, the plural ending is always **-і**.

Test your understanding with these questions:
...

<!-- SCOPE
Covers: Adjective agreement in gender (masculine, feminine, neuter) and number (plural) in the Nominative case, hard and soft stems.
Not covered:
  - Colors and Clothing → a1-12
  - Description: Adverbs → a1-42
